explanation: The requirement states that when in PCV or PSV mode, the operator can press and hold a single button to initiate an Inspiratory Pause (IP), which involves closing both the inspiratory and expiratory valve at the end of the inspiratory phase. This pause must last for as long as the button is held, but no longer than 40 seconds. To formalize this, we model that when the inspiratory pause button is pressed (inspiratoryPauseButton) and the ventilator is in either PCVMode or PSVMode, then at the end of the inspiratory phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), the inspiratory valve (inValveClose) and expiratory valve (outValveClose) are both closed, and the "inspiratoryPause" is active. This should persist while the button is held, but no longer than a fixed bound (here, N_DURATION=40). We use the template that ensures: whenever the condition (PCVMode or PSVMode) AND inspiratoryPauseButton is true at the end of the inspiratory phase, then within 40s, inspiratory pause is assured (with the corresponding valves closed), thus capturing the requirement.

The requirement specifies that in PCV and PSV modes, by pressing and holding a single button, the Inspiratory Pause is initiated at the end of inspiration and both valves (inspiratory and expiratory) are closed as long as the button is held, but not exceeding 40 seconds. The closest atomic propositions are: PCVMode and PSVMode (modes), inspiratoryPauseButton (operator input), inspiratoryPause (pause active), inValveClose and outValveClose (valve states), and inspiratoryPhaseEnd (end of inspiration). The critical logic: When (PCVMode or PSVMode) and inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed at the inspiratoryPhaseEnd, inspiratoryPause becomes active, and both inValveClose and outValveClose hold for as long as the button remains pressed, but not longer than 40 seconds. We capture this as: Whenever the inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed at the end of the inspiratory phase in either PCV or PSV mode, then for up to 40 seconds (or until the button is let go) inspiratoryPause, inValveClose, and outValveClose should be held true. We use template 12:
G (bool_exp2 -> ((G[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3) | (G bool_exp3)))
where bool_exp2 = (inspiratoryPhaseEnd & inspiratoryPauseButton & (PCVMode | PSVMode)), bool_exp3 = (inspiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose), and N_DURATION = 40. This expresses: Whenever an inspiration ends and the pause button is pressed in a valid mode, both valves are closed and the pause is active for up to 40 seconds (unless the button is released earlier, which is not modeled directly but is the maximum duration condition).

The requirement states that when the ventilator is in either PCV or PSV mode, the operator can press and hold the Inspiratory Pause button at the end of the inspiratory phase to initiate an Inspiratory Pause (IP), during which both the inspiratory and expiratory valves remain closed for up to 40 seconds, or as long as the button is held (whichever is shorter). This is captured as: whenever ('in PCV or PSV mode' AND 'inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed' AND 'end of inspiratory phase') is true, then for up to 40 seconds, both the inspiratory valve and expiratory valve should be closed (inValveClose & outValveClose). This is achieved using the template: G (bool_exp1 -> (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3)), which expresses that globally, whenever the triggering condition holds, then within 40 seconds, the resulting state must be achieved and maintained as long as allowed by the spec.

The requirement is that in PCV and PSV ventilation modes, the operator can hold the Inspiratory Pause button to close both inspiratory and expiratory valves at the end of the inspiratory phase for up to 40 seconds. We interpret this as: when in either PCV or PSV mode, if the inspiratory pause button is pressed at the end of the inspiratory phase, then both valves ('inValveClose' and 'outValveClose') must remain closed for the duration that the button is held, but not more than 40 seconds. In LTL, we describe the property that, whenever (PCVMode or PSVMode) and inspiratoryPauseButton and inspiratoryPhaseEnd are true, it must be the case that both valves are closed (inValveClose & outValveClose) for up to 40 seconds (N_DURATION = 40), or globally as long as inspiratoryPause is true and within the limit. This matches a response pattern guaranteeing the safety requirement within a maximum hold time.

The natural language requirement asks for the ability, in PCV and PSV modes, to initiate and maintain an Inspiratory Pause (IP) by holding down a button (and its effect is to close both inspiratory and expiratory valves) for up to 40 seconds. Given the atomic propositions, 'inspiratoryPauseButton' indicates when the operator holds the button, 'inValveClose' and 'outValveClose' indicate the closure of both valves, and 'inspiratoryPause' indicates the activation of the pause. In Pressure Controlled Ventilation (PCV) or Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV), holding the button should activate and maintain the inspiratory pause condition (i.e., both valves closed) for as long as the button is held, but no more than 40 seconds. The best way to formalize this is that whenever the inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed during PCV or PSV, the system will, for up to 40 seconds, hold the inspiratoryPause condition (which semantically includes closing both valves). The template that captures this is template 14: 'G (bool_exp2 -> (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3))', expressing that globally, if the relevant condition holds (here, being in PCV or PSV mode AND the inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed), then within 40 seconds, the inspiratoryPause will hold (i.e., the button triggers the pause action for up to 40s). N_DURATION is set to 40. We use 'PCVMode | PSVMode' to indicate either mode as the context for the button action.

The requirement is that in PCV and PSV modes, there should be a possibility to press a button (the inspiratoryPauseButton) that triggers and sustains an Inspiratory Pause (inspiratoryPause), where both inspiratory (inValveClose) and expiratory (outValveClose) valves are closed at the end of the inspiratory phase, and the pause is maintained as long as the operator holds the button, but never longer than 40 seconds. The LTL formula captures this as: Whenever (in PCVMode or PSVMode) and inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed at the end of the inspiratory phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), then within the next 40 time units (N_DURATION=40), the state where inspiratoryPause holds and both inValveClose and outValveClose are true must be realized. This covers the behavior that the Inspiratory Pause is initiated and sustained, and not holding longer than 40 seconds is implied by limiting the duration in the temporal operator.

The requirement states that when the ventilator is in either PCV (Pressure Controlled Ventilation) or PSV (Pressure Support Ventilation) mode, pressing and holding the inspiratory pause button should trigger an inspiratory pause, closing both inspiratory and expiratory valves, for up to but no longer than 40 seconds. Template 14 allows us to specify that globally, whenever the system is in PCV or PSV mode and the inspiratory pause button is pressed (bool_exp1), then within 40 seconds, the system should be in inspiratory pause and both valves closed (bool_exp3). To ensure all boolean expression placeholders are valid, bool_exp2 is set to 'True', which is always a valid boolean expression but will be ignored by this template. N_DURATION is set to 40 seconds as required.

The requirement specifies that when the ventilator operates in either PCV or PSV mode, pressing and holding the Inspiratory Pause button must initiate an Inspiratory Pause—closing both the inspiratory and expiratory valves—for as long as the button is held, but this state must not exceed 40 seconds. The translation encodes that whenever the system is in PCVMode or PSVMode and inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed, then within 40 seconds, inspiratoryPause is active and both inValveClose and outValveClose are true (all valves closed indicating the pause). This is captured using template 14, which expresses that a given trigger condition will eventually (within a bounded N_DURATION) lead to a required consequence. All boolean expressions are well-formed and use only specified atomic propositions.

The requirement specifies that in both PCV and PSV mode, the operator can hold a button to cause an Inspiratory Pause (IP), which closes both the inspiratory and expiratory valves at the end of the inspiratory phase, and the pause lasts as long as the button is held, but not more than 40 seconds. 

Translating to LTL, we capture the notion that whenever the operator (user_equalTo_operator) is operating the system AND (PCVMode OR PSVMode is active), if the inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed at the end of the inspiratory phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), then 'inspiratoryPause' is true, and both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveClose' are true for as long as the button is held, but for no more than 40 seconds (N_DURATION=40). As the templates only allow us to state properties about relations between button, pause, and valve signals, we can express: Whenever the inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed at the end of the inspiratory phase, then at the next time instant, inspiratoryPause AND inValveClose AND outValveClose hold, and, as long as the button is held, the condition persists, but not exceeding 40 seconds. This matches template 34: G (bool_exp1 -> (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3)), where bool_exp1 is 'inspiratoryPauseButton & inspiratoryPhaseEnd' and bool_exp3 is 'inspiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose'.

The requirement states that in PCV (Pressure Controlled Ventilation) and PSV (Pressure Support Ventilation) modes, there must be a single button for the operator to initiate an Inspiratory Pause (IP). Once pressed (and held), both inspiratory and expiratory valves should close at the end of the inspiratory phase, for the duration the button is held, but no longer than 40 seconds. The atomic propositions of most interest are: PCVMode (system is in PCV mode), PSVMode (system is in PSV mode), inspiratoryPauseButton (button is pressed), inValveClose (inspiratory valve is closed), outValveClose (expiratory valve is closed), inspiratoryPhaseEnd (end of inspiratory phase), and inspiratoryPause (indicates Inspiratory Pause). The temporal aspect ('as long as the operator holds but no longer than 40 sec') suggests, upon pressing the pause button at the end of inspiration, valves remain closed (Inspiratory Pause) for up to 40 seconds or until the button is released. Thus, whenever (PCVMode or PSVMode) and inspiratoryPauseButton and inspiratoryPhaseEnd are true (i.e., at the end of inspiration and pause is requested), then the system must enter and maintain the inspiratoryPause (both valves closed; indicated by inspiratoryPause) for up to 40 seconds, or until button is released, but never more than 40 seconds. The best fitting template is: G (bool_exp1 -> (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3)), i.e., whenever (PCV or PSV) & pause button & end of inspiration, then within 40 sec, inspiratoryPause (valves closed) will be true (and maintained as long as the operator holds, not exceeding 40 sec). In summary: always, if (PCVMode or PSVMode) and inspiratoryPauseButton and inspiratoryPhaseEnd occur, then within 40 seconds, inspiratoryPause (i.e., both valves closed) must hold for the duration the button is held, but not longer than 40 sec. N_DURATION=40 reflects the 40 second limit.
